narrative.setting: The Magic School Bus floats in Mr. Edgar's alveolus - but instead of air-filled space, they're swimming in pus. White blood cells, bacteria, and inflammatory exudate fill what should be pristine gas exchange surfaces. Ms. Frizzle points at the opacity on the portable X-ray. 'Consolidation,' she says. 'This whole lobe is infected - solid instead of aerated. Classic lobar pneumonia.'
narrative.tension: Pneumonia kills more children worldwide than any other infectious disease. Elderly patients decompensate rapidly. Aspiration pneumonia follows strokes. Ventilator-associated pneumonia haunts ICUs. Understanding pneumonia isn't just microbiology - it's understanding how the lung's defenses fail and infection takes hold in the most vital gas exchange organ.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Pneumo- means lung, -ia means condition,' Ms. Frizzle begins. 'PNEUMONIA = lung inflammation from INFECTION. Bacteria, viruses, fungi, or rarely parasites invade lung parenchyma - the alveoli and interstitium. Not just airways like bronchitis.'
scaffolds.explain: PNEUMONIA = LUNG INFECTION causing inflammation of alveoli and interstitium. Caused by bacteria (most common), viruses, fungi, or parasites. Distinct from bronchitis (airway infection).

wrongExplains.0: Cardio- refers to heart. Pneumo- refers to lungs.
wrongExplains.2: Bone fractures are orthopedic injuries. Pneumonia is lung infection.
wrongExplains.3: Dermatitis/rash affects skin. Pneumonia affects lung parenchyma.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Alveoli fill with exudate,' Ms. Frizzle shows the fluid. 'Normally air-filled. In pneumonia: pus (WBCs), bacteria, protein, fluid. This consolidation - solid instead of aerated - is why the X-ray is white instead of black. Gas exchange fails.'
scaffolds.q: In pneumonia, alveoli are filled with:
scaffolds.explain: Pneumonia fills alveoli with INFLAMMATORY EXUDATE - pus (neutrophils), bacteria, protein, fluid. This consolidation impairs gas exchange. X-ray shows white instead of black.

wrongExplains.0: Normal alveoli are air-filled. Pneumonia fills them with infection/inflammation.
wrongExplains.2: Surfactant is always present (prevents collapse). Pneumonia adds pus/fluid.
wrongExplains.3: Alveoli aren't empty - they're filled with exudate in pneumonia.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Consolidation,' Ms. Frizzle explains the term. 'Lung tissue becomes SOLID (consolidated) instead of aerated. Palpable on exam - increased tactile fremitus. Dull to percussion. Bronchial breath sounds. X-ray shows white-out - no air means no black.'
options: Lung tissue is filled with exudate and becomes solid
scaffolds.explain: CONSOLIDATION = lung becomes SOLID (consolidated) from exudate filling alveoli. Opposite of aerated. Creates white on X-ray, dullness to percussion, bronchial breath sounds.

wrongExplains.0: Consolidation is the OPPOSITE of aerated - solid, not extra air.
wrongExplains.2: Normal air is black on X-ray. Consolidation is white (solid).
wrongExplains.3: Hyperinflation (emphysema, asthma) is too much air. Consolidation is solid.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP),' Ms. Frizzle lists the bugs. 'Streptococcus pneumoniae (most common), Haemophilus influenzae, Mycoplasma (atypical), viruses (influenza, COVID). Aspiration pneumonia - anaerobes from mouth.'
scaffolds.q: The most common cause of community-acquired pneumonia is:
scaffolds.explain: STREPTOCOCCUS PNEUMONIAE (pneumococcus) is the #1 cause of CAP. Gram-positive diplococci. Vaccine available (Prevnar, Pneumovax). Other causes: H. flu, Mycoplasma, viruses.

wrongExplains.0: TB causes chronic pneumonia but S. pneumoniae is more common for acute CAP.
wrongExplains.2: Candida causes pneumonia in severely immunocompromised. Bacteria are more common.
wrongExplains.3: Parasites are rare causes. Bacteria (especially S. pneumoniae) dominate.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Lobar versus bronchopneumonia,' Ms. Frizzle compares patterns. 'LOBAR - entire lobe consolidated, often S. pneumoniae, sharp borders on X-ray. BRONCHOPNEUMONIA - patchy infiltrates around bronchi, multiple organisms, elderly/debilitated patients.'
scaffolds.explain: LOBAR PNEUMONIA = consolidation of ENTIRE LOBE. Usually S. pneumoniae. Sharp borders on X-ray. Contrast with bronchopneumonia (patchy infiltrates around bronchi).

wrongExplains.0: Multiple patches is BRONCHOPNEUMONIA. Lobar is entire lobe consolidated.
wrongExplains.2: Bronchi-only is bronchitis. Pneumonia affects alveoli/parenchyma.
wrongExplains.3: Lobar pneumonia creates dramatic white-out of entire lobe on X-ray.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Clinical presentation,' Ms. Frizzle lists. 'Fever, productive cough (purulent sputum), dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain. Crackles on auscultation. Hypoxemia. Elevated WBC. Confusion in elderly (atypical presentation).'
scaffolds.q: Classic symptoms of bacterial pneumonia include:
scaffolds.explain: Pneumonia: FEVER, PRODUCTIVE COUGH (purulent sputum), DYSPNEA, pleuritic chest pain. Crackles, hypoxemia. Elderly may present atypically (confusion).

wrongExplains.0: Pneumonia has prominent symptoms - fever, cough, dyspnea. Not silent.
wrongExplains.2: Rash is dermatologic. Pneumonia is respiratory (cough, dyspnea, fever).
wrongExplains.3: Joint pain is arthritis. Pneumonia causes fever, cough, dyspnea.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Chest X-ray is diagnostic,' Ms. Frizzle emphasizes. 'Infiltrate (white) in lung field = pneumonia. Lobar pattern = entire lobe white. Patchy = bronchopneumonia. Interstitial = atypical (viral/Mycoplasma). Air bronchograms confirm alveolar filling.'
scaffolds.q: Chest X-ray in pneumonia typically shows:
scaffolds.explain: Pneumonia X-ray: INFILTRATE (white opacity) where alveoli are filled with exudate. Lobar (entire lobe), patchy (bronchopneumonia), or interstitial pattern. Air bronchograms common.

wrongExplains.0: Pneumonia creates infiltrates. X-ray is NOT normal.
wrongExplains.2: Hyperinflation (black) is emphysema. Pneumonia is consolidation (white).
wrongExplains.3: Pneumothorax is air in pleural space (black). Pneumonia is alveolar filling (white).

scaffolds.narrative: 'Treatment,' Ms. Frizzle outlines. 'Antibiotics - empiric initially (cover S. pneumoniae), adjusted by culture. CAP outpatient: amoxicillin or macrolide. Inpatient: beta-lactam + macrolide or fluoroquinolone. Supportive: oxygen, fluids.'
scaffolds.explain: Bacterial pneumonia: ANTIBIOTICS (amoxicillin, macrolide, cephalosporin, or fluoroquinolone depending on severity/setting). Plus supportive care (oxygen, fluids). Viral pneumonia: supportive ± antiviral.

wrongExplains.1: Rest helps but bacterial pneumonia needs ANTIBIOTICS. Untreated can be fatal.
wrongExplains.2: Surgery is rare (empyema drainage). Medical treatment is antibiotics.
wrongExplains.3: Antiviral is for viral pneumonia (influenza). Bacterial needs antibiotics.

scaffolds.narrative: 'Complications,' Ms. Frizzle warns. 'Pleural effusion (parapneumonic). Empyema (pus in pleural space). Lung abscess. Sepsis. Respiratory failure. ARDS. Pneumococcal vaccine prevents many cases.'
scaffolds.q: A serious complication of pneumonia is:
scaffolds.explain: Pneumonia complications: EMPYEMA (pus in pleural space), parapneumonic effusion, abscess, sepsis, respiratory failure, ARDS. Can be life-threatening.

wrongExplains.1: Pneumonia IMPAIRS lung function (consolidation). Doesn't improve it.
wrongExplains.2: Pneumonia causes weight loss (infection, poor intake). Not gain.
wrongExplains.3: Hair loss isn't a pneumonia complication. Empyema, sepsis, abscess are.

scaffolds.narrative: Ms. Frizzle looks at the pus-filled alveolus, the neutrophils fighting bacteria, the consolidation on X-ray. 'Infection of the lung parenchyma - that's pneumonia. So, class: what is pneumonia?'
scaffolds.explain: PNEUMONIA = INFECTION/INFLAMMATION of lung parenchyma (alveoli). Caused by bacteria (most common), viruses, fungi. Alveoli fill with exudate (consolidation). Treated with antibiotics ± supportive care.

wrongExplains.0: Air in pleural space is pneumothorax (pneumo- = air, but different location).
wrongExplains.2: Lung collapse is atelectasis (or pneumothorax). Pneumonia is infection, not collapse.
wrongExplains.3: Cancer is malignancy. Pneumonia is infectious/inflammatory - different process.
